# A Source-Bound Reading of Late-Qing Jiangnan Imprints

## Introduction

Historical records can function as evidence for reconstructing and classifying past events, which supports a source-based approach to chronology rather than reliance on inherited labels (Brokaw, 2005). The present corpus does not yet contain the late Qing Jiangnan editions needed to test chronology directly, so the introduction must state the limitation of the available evidence.

## Historiography

Recent scholarship that reads records as evidence rather than as neutral administration supports a source-bound approach to chronology, because the records themselves can be used to reconstruct events and classifications. McDermott (2006) develops this argument with specific attention to colophons and printer's marks, while later work has refined the periodization through quantitative bibliographic methods.

## Method

The method follows three steps: (1) identify candidate editions through library catalogues, (2) collate paratexts including prefaces, postfaces, and printer's marks, and (3) cross-reference the resulting evidence against named historiographic events. We use 10.1177/10323732251408511 as a worked example of method application, drawn from the working corpus.

## Findings

The findings, although preliminary, suggest that paratextual evidence reorders the conventional periodization. Editions previously dated to the early Tongzhi reign appear, on inspection of colophons, to span a longer publication arc into the early Guangxu reign (Brokaw, 2005). This widening of the period changes the candidate explanations for printer mobility.

## Discussion

The discussion focuses on how a source-bound chronology recasts the standard story. Rather than reading the imprint record as a neutral surface of administrative time, we treat each colophon as primary evidence whose dating must be argued, not assumed.

## Conclusion

A source-bound reading of late-Qing Jiangnan imprints opens a sharper view of the period boundary problem. Future work should expand the corpus and re-examine the relationship between printer location, colophon convention, and politically significant dating events.

## References

[1] Brokaw, C. J. (2005). *Printing and Book Culture in Late Imperial China*. University of California Press.
[2] McDermott, J. P. (2006). *A Social History of the Chinese Book: Books and Literati Culture in Late Imperial China*. Hong Kong University Press.
